A Slow May Morning, Courtesy of a Robin Alarm Clock

Thursday, May 28, 2026 · 4 species· 13 detections

Steller's jay is a bird native to western North America and the mountains of Central America, closely related to the blue jay found in eastern North America. It is the only crested jay west of the Rocky Mountains.

Look, I didn't ask to be awake before six. The neighborhood robin, apparently, had opinions about that — and once you're up, you're up. So out to the porch I went, coffee in hand, ears doing the heavy lifting.

The first real gift of the morning was an Olive-sided Flycatcher. If you've never heard one, picture a bird that sounds like it's ordering a round at the bar — that emphatic quick-three-beers! whistle cutting clean through the dawn. They're long-distance migrants, wintering down in the Andes, and every spring I forget how much I miss that voice until it comes back. This one was in fine form, holding court from somewhere upslope and refusing to be ignored. eBird says others nearby have been hearing them too, which tracks — feels like a little pulse of them moving through.

The Steller's Jays were, of course, doing their Steller's Jay thing. Loud, opinionated, very pleased with themselves. I love them but they are not subtle birds, and on a morning where I was hoping for something a bit more contemplative, they were essentially the brass section warming up before anyone else got to play. Their scrubbier cousins chimed in briefly from lower down — a nice little jay sandwich, conifers up top, oaks below.

And then, almost as a footnote, a Warbling Vireo. Just the one burst of that tumbling, run-on song — the kind of phrase that always sounds like the bird is trying to talk itself into something. Vireos have this quality where if you're not paying attention you'll miss them entirely, and on a quiet day like this, that one little cascade of notes felt like the morning's real reward.

That was about it. No warbler fallout, no surprise flyovers, no drama. Just a handful of voices in the half-light and a robin somewhere still very proud of waking me up. Some mornings the list is long and you're scribbling to keep up; some mornings you sit with four species and a lukewarm cup of coffee and call it good.

Today was the second kind. I'll take it.

Tomorrow: earplugs. Probably. (No I won't.)

Today's cast

The California scrub jay is a species of scrub jay native to western North America. It ranges from southern British Columbia throughout California and western Nevada near Reno to west of the Sierra Nevada.
